title: Copying and Moving Files
intro: Your project has files scattered in the wrong places -- a config file sitting in the components folder, a README that needs to live in two directories. Let's master the cp and mv commands so you can copy and move files exactly where they belong.

teach.title: cp = Photocopying a Document
teach.body: Imagine you need the same report in two different filing cabinets. You walk to the copier, make a duplicate, and put the copy in the second cabinet. The original stays exactly where it was. **The `cp` command works the same way** -- it creates an identical copy of a file and puts it wherever you specify, while the original remains untouched.

teach.title: mv = Physically Relocating a Box
teach.body: When you move apartments, you pick up a box from the old place and carry it to the new place. The box is gone from the old spot and now lives at the new address. **The `mv` command does exactly this** -- it removes the file from its current location and places it in the new one. Bonus: if you 'move' a file to the same directory with a different name, you've just renamed it -- like relabeling a box without carrying it anywhere.

teach.title: cp and mv: Copy vs Move
teach.body:
Two commands, two different outcomes:

- **`cp source destination`** -- Creates a duplicate. Original stays. Now you have two copies.
- **`mv source destination`** -- Relocates the file. Original is gone. Only one copy exists.
- **`cp -r folder/ newFolder/`** -- Copy an entire directory (the `-r` flag means recursive -- it copies everything inside).
- **`mv oldname.js newname.js`** -- Rename a file by 'moving' it to the same location with a new name.

The key difference: after `cp`, you have two files. After `mv`, you have one.

teach.title: Common Patterns
teach.code:
# Copy a file into another directory
cp config.json backup/config.json

# Copy an entire folder
cp -r src/ src-backup/

# Move a file to a different folder
mv todo.txt docs/todo.txt

# Rename a file (move to same dir, new name)
mv app.js index.js
teach.annotation: Notice how renaming is just mv with a new name in the same directory. There's no separate 'rename' command -- mv handles both moving and renaming.

teach.body: Knowing cp and mv lets you reorganize AI-generated projects in seconds instead of dragging files around in a GUI. When a client asks you to restructure their codebase, you can do it in one terminal session -- that's vibecoding efficiency that earns you more per hour.
Use cp to duplicate files (original stays) and mv to relocate or rename files (original is gone). Add -r to cp when copying directories. Renaming is just mv with a new filename in the same location.

steps.instruction: The project needs a backup of config.json before we make changes. Copy it to a file called config.backup.json in the same directory.
steps.hint: Use 'cp source destination' to create a copy. Both files will be in the current directory.

steps.instruction: The README.md file needs to be in the docs/ folder instead. First create the docs folder, then move the file there.
steps.hint: Create the directory with mkdir, then use mv to relocate the file. Chain them with &&.

steps.instruction: We need a complete backup of the src/ directory. Copy it recursively to a new folder called src-backup/.
steps.hint: Use 'cp -r' to copy a directory and everything inside it.

steps.instruction: The file src/utils.js should actually be called src/helpers.js. Rename it using mv.
steps.hint: Rename by using mv with the old name and new name in the same directory.

exercise.completionMessage: You've mastered the basics of copying and moving! You backed up a config file, relocated a README, duplicated an entire directory, and renamed a file -- all without leaving the terminal. These commands are the foundation of project organization.
